title: Roast beef sirloin
subtitle: Salt-rubbed, rested, served pink.
excerpt: A whole sirloin roasted to medium-rare, rested for as long as it cooks, served pink with Yorkshire puddings, roast potatoes, and a red-wine gravy built from the pan. Sunday roast for six to eight.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136, chapter on roast beef and the doctrine of long resting. USDA Food Safety and Inspection Service guidance on beef internal temperatures (medium-rare 57°C, medium 63°C). Hannah Glasse, The Art of Cookery Made Plain and Easy (1747), public domain, on dripping and Yorkshire puddings cooked under the meat.

recipe.freezeNotes: Cooked slices freeze up to two months wrapped tight in foil and a freezer bag. Defrost overnight in the fridge; reheat slices in a covered dish with a spoonful of beef stock at 140°C for 15 minutes, no longer or the meat overcooks.
recipe.batchNotes: The cut is what it is; doubling means two joints not one bigger one, and two roasting pans. The oven needs room around each joint for the heat to circulate.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Salt-rub the joint up to 24 hours ahead and leave uncovered on a rack in the fridge — the surface dries and browns faster. Bring back to room temperature for an hour before it goes in the oven.
recipe.temperatureNote: screaming hot to start, then dropped to 160°C for the long roast

recipeTools.notes: Heavy-based, big enough for the joint with a finger's width of clearance on every side.
Lifts the meat above the fat so the underside browns.
The whole recipe rests on hitting 54°C in the centre.

glossaryTerms.definition: The continued rise in internal temperature after a roast leaves the oven — a beef joint resting on the side typically gains 3–5°C in the first ten minutes before levelling off. Pull the joint earlier than the target.

content.text: A 1.8 kg boned and rolled sirloin, salt-rubbed the day before, roasted hot for fifteen minutes and then slow at 160°C until the centre reads 54°C. Rested for thirty minutes on a warm plate while the Yorkshires bake and the gravy comes together. Carved into thick slices across the grain, served pink. Six to eight.

Sirloin is the cut that wants to be roasted: tight grain, a fat cap that bastes the meat as it cooks, enough internal marbling to stay tender at medium-rare. The fillet is leaner and easier to overcook; the rib is grander and asks more of the cook. The sirloin sits in the middle and rewards a careful eye on the thermometer.

attrs.title: Internal temperatures
attrs.body: Pull from the oven at 54°C in the deepest part of the joint for medium-rare; the rest takes it to 58°C, the doneness Beeton calls 'pink, with the juice running clear amber'. For medium, pull at 60°C and rest to 64°C. For well done, choose a different cut: sirloin tightens past medium and loses the reason for buying it.

content.text: What you need

items.prepNote: boned and rolled, fat cap intact
a sturdy red, not the cheapest

content.text: A heavy roasting pan with a rack is the only specialist kit. An instant-read thermometer is the difference between a sirloin you carve with pride and one you slice through with regret. Buy the cheapest probe thermometer the kitchen shop sells; it pays for itself on the first joint.
Method
Salt the joint the day before

Pat the sirloin dry with kitchen paper. Mix {{sea-salt-flakes}} of sea salt flakes with {{black-pepper}} of cracked black pepper and {{english-mustard-powder}} of English mustard powder. Rub the mixture over the joint, including the fat cap. Sit the joint on a rack over a tray and leave uncovered in the fridge for at least eight hours, ideally a full day. The surface dries, the salt penetrates, and the crust browns hard in the oven.
Bring to room temperature

Take the joint out of the fridge one hour before it goes in the oven. A cold-from-the-fridge joint gives you a grey band of overcooked meat around a cold centre. An hour on the side narrows that band considerably.
Sear in a hot oven

Heat the oven to 220°C (200°C fan). Scatter the {{onion}} halved onions, {{carrot}} chunked carrots, and {{celery}} chunked celery across the base of the roasting pan with the {{garlic}} of crushed garlic and the {{thyme}} of thyme leaves. Rub the joint with {{rapeseed-oil}} of rapeseed oil. Sit it fat-side-up on the rack above the vegetables. Roast for fifteen minutes — the surface browns hard.
Drop the heat and roast slow

Turn the oven down to 160°C (140°C fan). Roast for a further 45 to 55 minutes for a 1.8 kg joint. Start checking the centre at 40 minutes; the thermometer goes into the thickest part, away from any bone. Pull when the reading hits 54°C for medium-rare. The crust will be deep mahogany; the meat under the crust still firm but giving.
Rest, then carve

Lift the joint onto a warm plate, tent loosely with foil, and rest for 30 minutes. The juices that flooded the cut edges retreat back into the muscle fibres; the internal temperature rises another 4°C through
carryover cooking
, arriving at a perfect 58°C in the centre. Thirty minutes is long. Use the time for the Yorkshires and the gravy. Carve into 5 mm slices, across the grain, with a long, thin-bladed knife.
Build the gravy in the pan

While the joint rests, set the roasting pan over a medium hob, vegetables and all. Pour in {{red-wine}} of red wine and scrape the brown bits off the base with a wooden spoon. Reduce by half, around five minutes. Pour in {{stock-beef}} of hot beef stock. Simmer for ten minutes. If a thicker gravy is wanted, slake {{plain-flour}} of plain flour in a little cold water, whisk it into the simmering liquid, and cook for two more minutes. Strain through a sieve into a jug, pressing the vegetables to extract every drop.
Troubleshooting

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Most failures are temperature failures — either the oven, the meat, or both.

items.cause: Pulled too late, or no thermometer used. Carryover cooking adds 3–5°C after the joint leaves the oven.
items.fix: Use a probe. Pull at 54°C for medium-rare and rest.

items.cause: Surface still damp when it went in, or starting oven not hot enough.
items.fix: Salt-rub the day before, dry uncovered in the fridge, and start the oven at 220°C.

items.cause: Joint went in straight from the fridge.
items.fix: Always rest at room temperature for an hour first.

items.cause: Not enough fond on the pan base, or not enough reduction.
items.fix: Roast on a bed of vegetables that brown alongside the meat. Reduce the wine by half before adding stock.

items.symptom: Joint sliced into and bled across the board
items.fix: Rest a full 30 minutes for a 1.8 kg joint. The board stays clean and the slices stay pink.

content.text: Make ahead, freezing, leftovers

Salt-rub the joint up to 24 hours ahead. The gravy can be made up to a day ahead from a roast-beef stock the week before; warm and finish on the day. Cooked slices freeze for two months; defrost slowly and reheat gently with a spoon of stock. Leftover beef makes the best sandwich the next day — sourdough, horseradish, watercress, a smear of mustard. Or shred and warm through the leftover gravy into a hash with fried potatoes.
Where this dish lives

The roast sirloin is the centrepiece of the English Sunday lunch and has been since Henry VIII reputedly knighted a particularly fine loin at Hoghton Tower. Beeton's 1861 recipe insists on the long resting and the red gravy; Hannah Glasse a century earlier cooked the Yorkshires under the joint to catch the dripping. The combination of beef, Yorkshire, gravy, and potatoes is the architecture of a British weekend. Serve with goose-fat roast potatoes, honey-roast parsnips, a heap of seasonal greens, and the gravy in a warm jug.
